title: Isolated prolonged PT: factor VII deficiency
vignette:
A 12-year-old boy experiences excessive bleeding following a tooth extraction. His past medical history includes episodes of painful joint swelling from minor trauma. Physical examination shows a soft and nontender abdomen with a liver span of 10 cm. The spleen is not palpable. Laboratory results are as follows:
Bleeding time: 5 min
Activated partial thromboplastin time: 25 sec
Prothrombin time: 23 sec
This patient most likely has a deficiency involving which of the following factors?

clues.text: excessive bleeding following a tooth extraction
clues.note: Bleeding provoked by a surgical/hemostatic challenge (tooth extraction) signals a bleeding disorder. Combined with the lab pattern, it points to a clotting-factor problem rather than a vessel or platelet issue.

clues.text: painful joint swelling from minor trauma
clues.note: Hemarthrosis (painful joint bleeding) is DEEP-TISSUE bleeding, characteristic of a COAGULATION FACTOR deficiency (coagulopathy), as opposed to the mucocutaneous bleeding seen with platelet/von Willebrand disorders.
A normal bleeding time (normal ~2-7 min) indicates adequate platelet number and function and normal von Willebrand factor - so this is not a platelet/vWF problem.
A normal aPTT (normal ~25-40 sec) indicates an intact INTRINSIC pathway (factors VIII, IX, XI, XII). This rules out hemophilia A/B and the other intrinsic-factor deficiencies.

An isolated prolonged PT (normal ~11-15 sec) with a normal aPTT localizes the defect to the EXTRINSIC pathway. Factor VII is the only factor unique to the extrinsic pathway, so factor VII deficiency is the answer.

whatMatters: Boil it down: deep-tissue/joint bleeding (a coagulopathy) with a NORMAL bleeding time, NORMAL aPTT (25 / normal ~25-40), and an ISOLATED prolonged PT (23 / normal ~11-15). An isolated prolonged PT points to the EXTRINSIC pathway, and factor VII is the only factor unique to it - so this is factor VII deficiency. Answer A.
strategy: Localize a bleeding disorder with the three screening tests. Platelet/vWF problems -> prolonged bleeding time + mucocutaneous bleeding. Coagulation-factor problems -> deep-tissue/joint bleeding + prolonged PT and/or aPTT. Then: isolated high PT = extrinsic = factor VII; isolated high aPTT = intrinsic (VIII, IX, XI, XII); both high = common pathway (X, V, II, fibrinogen).

eliminations.label: factor VIII (hemophilia A) - would prolong aPTT (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: factor VIII deficiency is hemophilia A (X-linked), causing hemarthroses and deep bleeding. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: factor VIII is in the INTRINSIC pathway, so it prolongs the aPTT - but this patient's aPTT is NORMAL (25); the abnormal test is the PT. The trap is matching 'joint bleeding' to hemophilia without checking which test is abnormal. ✅ Correct if: prolonged aPTT with a normal PT and normal bleeding time.

eliminations.label: factor XI (hemophilia C) - intrinsic, normal here
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: factor XI deficiency is hemophilia C (autosomal recessive), with bleeding mainly after surgery/trauma. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: factor XI is intrinsic and prolongs the aPTT, which is normal here; the abnormal test is the PT. ✅ Correct if: a prolonged aPTT with a normal PT.

eliminations.label: Hageman factor (XII) - prolongs aPTT but NO bleeding
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: factor XII (Hageman factor) deficiency prolongs the aPTT but causes NO clinical bleeding (it can even predispose to thrombosis). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it affects the aPTT (normal here) and wouldn't explain bleeding at all. ✅ Correct if: an incidentally prolonged aPTT with no bleeding history.

eliminations.label: von Willebrand factor - prolongs bleeding time, mucocutaneous
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: von Willebrand factor deficiency is the most common inherited bleeding disorder, with MUCOCUTANEOUS bleeding and a prolonged bleeding time (sometimes a high aPTT from low factor VIII). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this patient has a NORMAL bleeding time and DEEP (joint) bleeding; the abnormal test is the PT, which vWD doesn't isolate. ✅ Correct if: mucocutaneous bleeding with a prolonged bleeding time.

reasoning: Deep-tissue/joint bleeding (a coagulopathy) provoked by tooth extraction
Normal bleeding time (platelets/vWF intact) and normal aPTT (intrinsic intact)
Isolated prolonged PT = extrinsic pathway defect
Factor VII is the only factor unique to the extrinsic pathway -> answer A
mechanism: The PT measures the EXTRINSIC and common pathways; the aPTT measures the INTRINSIC and common pathways; the bleeding time reflects platelet/vWF function. Factor VII is the ONLY clotting factor unique to the extrinsic pathway, so its deficiency produces an ISOLATED prolonged PT with a normal aPTT and normal bleeding time. Coagulation-factor deficiencies cause deep-tissue and joint bleeding (hemarthroses), in contrast to the mucocutaneous bleeding of platelet/vWF disorders. Factor VII also has the shortest half-life of the clotting factors, which is why the PT rises first in early warfarin therapy and in liver disease.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Hematology > coagulation cascade and lab interpretation: PT (extrinsic, factor VII) vs aPTT (intrinsic) vs bleeding time (platelets/vWF); factor VII has the shortest half-life.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Read the three tests as a grid: which is abnormal? Only the PT here -> extrinsic -> factor VII. Don't let 'joint bleeding' lock you onto hemophilia before checking the aPTT.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on PT vs aPTT vs bleeding time, extrinsic/intrinsic/common pathway, hemarthrosis, coagulopathy vs platelet disorder? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Factor VIII/hemophilia (B) is the trap because of the joint bleeding - but hemophilia prolongs the aPTT, which is normal here. The isolated prolonged PT is factor VII.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank the rule: isolated high PT = factor VII; isolated high aPTT = intrinsic factor; both high = common pathway.

keywords.front: Isolated prolonged PT (normal aPTT, normal bleeding time)

firstAid.detail: Heme > PT (extrinsic, factor VII) vs aPTT (intrinsic) vs bleeding time; isolated prolonged PT = factor VII deficiency.
firstAid.location: Heme > PT (extrinsic, factor VII) vs aPTT (intrinsic) vs bleeding time; isolated prolonged PT = factor VII deficiency.

firstAid.detail: Heme > hemophilia A (VIII), B (IX), C (XI), factor XII (Hageman); von Willebrand disease.
firstAid.location: Heme > hemophilia A (VIII), B (IX), C (XI), factor XII (Hageman); von Willebrand disease.

firstAid.detail: Heme > extrinsic/intrinsic/common pathways; factor VII shortest half-life (warfarin, liver disease raise PT first).
firstAid.location: Heme > extrinsic/intrinsic/common pathways; factor VII shortest half-life (warfarin, liver disease raise PT first).

memoryHook: A lonely prolonged PT = factor SEVEN, the EXtrinsic loner. Joint bleeding but a normal aPTT? Not hemophilia - it's factor VII.
